original_abstract: A relatively broad consolidated consensus has emerged among experts regarding the competencies that should be fostered through an education for sustainable development at the higher education level. However, there is little empiri- cal support to aid in answering the question of which competencies should be promoted from the perspective of stu- dents and graduates. This was the main purpose for analyzing the corresponding results of the evaluation of the study programs in sustainable development at the University of Bern. In a standardized survey, students (N = 124), graduates (N = 121), and the supervisors of internships (N = 37) were asked, among other questions, how important they consider the fostering of the respective 13 competencies during their studies and for their professional activities. Overall, the
humanized_abstract: Experts’ consensus on key competences in education for sustainable development (ESD) is still fairly broad, particularly at the higher education level, although it has not yet been empirically validated and based on students and graduates’ opinions. The competences required should be promoted at ESD study programs, which is a central research question for this paper. Within the framework of the evaluation of the University of Bern study programs in sustainable development, students (N=124), graduates (N=121), and their internship supervisors (N=37) took part in a structured questionnaire. In addition to answering a number of predefined questions, they also assessed the importance of acquiring the 13 competences addressed by this study, within the context of their studies as well as their professional work. In general, all respondents rated these key competences as being highly relevant.

document.output: Experts’ consensus on key competences in education for sustainable development (ESD) is still fairly broad, particularly at the higher education level, although it has not yet been empirically validated and based on students and graduates’ opinions. The competences required should be promoted at ESD study programs, which is a central research question for this paper. Within the framework of the evaluation of the University of Bern study programs in sustainable development, students (N=124), graduates (N=121), and their internship supervisors (N=37) took part in a structured questionnaire. In addition to answering a number of predefined questions, they also assessed the importance of acquiring the 13 competences addressed by this study, within the context of their studies as well as their professional work. In general, all respondents rated these key competences as being highly relevant.
